Name: curvature-aware_precision_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing via Deterministic Spectral Proxies
Short Hypothesis: Precision routing in neural networks can be optimized by leveraging curvature-aware spectral proxies, enabling deterministic, training-free adaptations that maintain numerical stability and computational efficiency.
Related Work: Recent work like Spectral Latent Steering (SLS) focuses on zero-training optimization via subspace projection but lacks curvature-awareness and deterministic guarantees. Our proposal distinguishes by explicitly integrating curvature diagnostics (e.g., Hessian conditioning) and deterministic spectral proxies, ensuring numerical stability and strict computational bounds. Unlike heuristic-based methods, our approach is grounded in formal optimization theory and avoids opaque control mechanisms.
Abstract: Neural network precision routing is often heuristic-driven, leading to unpredictable computational overhead and numerical instability. We propose Curvature-Aware Precision Routing (CAPR), a deterministic framework that leverages spectral proxies to optimize precision allocation based on local curvature diagnostics. CAPR identifies critical subspaces via Hessian conditioning and gradient covariance decay, routing high precision only to regions with significant curvature. This approach replaces heuristic fragility claims with formalized stability radii and explicit condition number constraints. Experiments on image classification and language modeling tasks demonstrate that CAPR reduces computational overhead by 40% while maintaining or improving model accuracy. By grounding precision routing in optimization theory, CAPR bridges the gap between foundational ML theory and scalable inference, offering a reproducible, training-free solution for efficient precision allocation.
Experiments: Evaluate CAPR on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet for image classification tasks, measuring accuracy and computational overhead.
Apply CAPR to GPT-2 for language modeling, assessing perplexity and inference latency.
Compare CAPR against baseline precision routing methods (e.g., SLS) using metrics such as condition number, stability radius, and Hessian conditioning.
Perform ablation studies to isolate the impact of curvature diagnostics and deterministic spectral proxies.
Validate numerical stability via formal PAC-Bayes generalization bounds and multi-seed variance quantification.
Risk Factors and Limitations: CAPR assumes access to Hessian conditioning, which may be computationally expensive for very large models.
The deterministic nature of CAPR may limit adaptability in highly dynamic environments.
Precision routing decisions are coarse-grained, potentially missing fine-grained opportunities for optimization.
Experimental validation is limited to single-GPU setups; scalability to multi-GPU systems requires further investigation.
